summary: Backend-only test run for Sentinel-SRE multi-tenant SaaS. 18/19 tests passing. One real backend bug found: cross-request Prometheus webhook dedup does not consider severity, so alerts with same service+alertname but different severities get merged into a single incident (violates requirement).

critical.issue: Fingerprint dedup bug: within a single payload alerts are correctly bucketed by service::alertname::severity, but when looking up an existing open incident to append to, the code at main.py:414 only matches on service+alertname (metric), ignoring severity. Result: two webhook calls for (orders-svc, HighCPU, critical) then (orders-svc, HighCPU, warning) produce ONE incident instead of two. Fix: extend the `recent_incident = next(...)` filter to also compare `sig.get('signal_severity') == sev`.

action_items: Fix severity-aware dedup in /api/v1/telemetry/prometheus ingest: update the `recent_incident` lookup (main.py ~line 414) to also match `signal_severity` so different-severity alerts don't get merged into one incident.
Consider documenting that register/login emails are lowercased server-side (email with uppercase letters returns user.email in lowercase).
critical_code_review_comments: main.py line 414: the fingerprint dedup logic is inconsistent. New incidents are bucketed using `service::alertname::mapped_severity` but the existing-incident lookup ignores severity — this is the root cause of the failing fingerprint test.
main.py has 1355 lines — exceeds the 700-line guideline. Split into routers (auth_router, incidents_router, telemetry_router, chatops_router) and service modules for postmortem/dispatch formatting.
Duplicate `DispatchRequest` class defined twice (lines 525-527 and 569-571). Remove the duplicate.
`chatops_activity_log` is a process-global in-memory list (line 1327). In a multi-worker deployment this will desync and silently drop events. Persist ChatOps log to DB.

`_seed_admin_and_backfill` assigns ALL orphan incidents to the configured ADMIN_EMAIL user on every startup — fine for migration but leaves a footgun if ADMIN_EMAIL is ever changed in env (a future admin could accidentally inherit legacy data). Consider running the backfill only once via a migration flag.

Admin login with env `ADMIN_PASSWORD` works, but the admin webhook token printed at startup is visible in server logs only — acceptable for dev, but a production deployment should stream this once to stdout and never again.
Several business endpoints call `run_hybrid_analysis` synchronously inside postmortem generation; OK because wrapped in `asyncio.to_thread`, but note this can block if Chroma is slow.

Rate limit is keyed on `get_remote_address` (client IP). Behind a proxy/load balancer without trusted-proxy config this all collapses to the proxy IP — per-user limiting won't work. Consider keying by user_id where authenticated.
test_credentials: Admin seeded by startup: admin@sentinel.local / admin12345 (from env). Tests create TEST_ prefixed users at example.com.
seed_data_creation: Several TEST_ prefixed users and incidents created in /app/backend/incidents.db. Main agent will clean up per instruction.
context_for_next_testing_agent: Backend is started manually (not via supervisor) because supervisor points to a different default app. Start command: `cd /app/backend && JWT_SECRET=a-32-char-test-secret-for-testing-1234 DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///./incidents.db ALLOWED_ORIGINS='*' ADMIN_EMAIL=admin@sentinel.local ADMIN_PASSWORD=admin12345 nohup uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8001 > /tmp/backend.log 2>&1 &`. Always delete incidents.db and chroma_db and restart before running rate-limit tests (slowapi state is in-memory). Tests run as two invocations: the main suite excluding TestRateLimit (to keep quota available), then TestRateLimit alone. Email must be a public TLD (example.com) — email-validator rejects `.local`. Backend lowercases emails on register.
rca of the issue: Webhook dedup bug RCA — reproduction: (1) POST to /api/v1/telemetry/prometheus/{token} with alert {service=orders-svc, alertname=HighCPU, severity=critical}; (2) POST again with severity=warning. Expected: two incidents (different fingerprints orders-svc::HighCPU::severe vs ::moderate). Actual: one incident, second call returns the same incident_id. Root cause: main.py line 414 `recent_incident = next((inc for inc in open_incidents if any(sig.get('metric') == alertname ...)))` — the severity (`sig['signal_severity']`) is stored on the signal record but not used in the lookup filter. Mitigation: change the predicate to `sig.get('metric') == alertname and sig.get('signal_severity') == sev`. Also consider storing a denormalized `fingerprint` column on Incident and matching on that for clarity.
